title: Dog Panting Heavily at Rest: Triage Guide and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog panting while resting? Heavy panting without exercise is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn what to do and when to seek care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Heavy panting while resting is a life-threatening veterinary emergency that indicates your dog is struggling for oxygen or in significant distress. Seek immediate medical attention if your dog pants while inactive or has blue-tinted gums.

What does it mean if my dog is panting heavily while resting?

Heavy panting at rest occurs when a dog breathes rapidly with an open mouth while in a relaxed environment, cool temperature, or during sleep. Unlike the normal cooling mechanism used after a walk or play, panting while the body is inactive indicates that the dog is struggling to maintain normal oxygen levels or is experiencing significant internal distress.

Is heavy panting at rest a high-priority veterinary emergency?

  • Recognize that heavy panting while resting is a high-priority emergency, as it often indicates serious conditions like congestive heart failure, pulmonary edema, heatstroke, or severe internal pain.
  • Check your dog's tongue for a bluish or purple tint and observe if they are using abdominal muscles to breathe.
  • Transport your dog to an emergency hospital immediately if you notice these signs of respiratory distress.

How can providing a photo or video of my dog's breathing help the vet?

  • Capture a video or photo of your dog's breathing to provide life-saving data during the triage process.
  • Allow veterinary professionals to quickly assess breathing effort, respiration rate, and gum color from the visual.
  • Help the medical team prepare oxygen therapy or life-saving equipment before you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In emergency triage, labored breathing or excessive panting at rest warrants immediate transfer to the treatment area. Increased respiratory rate (tachypnea) and effort (dyspnea) are key indicators. While some dogs may exhibit respiratory crackles and wheezes, their absence does not rule out pulmonary edema. If the patient is stable, consider sending the dog home for the owner to monitor the sleeping respiratory rate (SRR) in a cool environment. A normal SRR is less than 30 breaths/minute; values exceeding this indicate tachypnea and warrant consideration for treatment such as furosemide at a dosage of at least 2 mg/kg, PO, bid.

Chapter: Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 113)

faqs.question: Could my dog just be stressed or anxious?
faqs.answer: While anxiety can cause panting, a medical emergency must be ruled out first when a dog pants at rest. Medical causes for respiratory distress are far more time-sensitive and dangerous than behavioral ones.

faqs.question: What should I do on the way to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the car cool with air conditioning, keep the dog as calm as possible, and call the clinic ahead of time to let them know you are arriving with a respiratory emergency.

faqs.question: Does a dog panting at rest always mean heart failure?
faqs.answer: Not always, but it is a common sign. Other possibilities include pneumonia, anemia, poisoning, or severe pain from an injury or bloat.
